https://github.com/cupy/cupy/issues/8541

# Background

Recently Leo added a patch to improve the Conda building experience in PR: https://github.com/cupy/cupy/pull/7603 (which is fantastic as this was [a large patch in the cupy-feedstock]( https://github.com/conda-forge/cupy-feedstock/blob/6db86965e825788262d22a5f3c8b9aa6b93f9d96/recipe/support_new_cuda_layout.diff ). Thanks Leo! 🙏)

This worked ok in our testing of CuPy 13.3.0 builds (not to mention previous CuPy builds where we applied this patch in conda-forge). However (as later identified after further investigation), this broke due to the recently released Setuptools [72.2.0]( https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/releases/tag/v72.2.0 ).

In particular the breakage resulted in the following error [on CI]( https://dev.azure.com/conda-forge/feedstock-builds/_build/results?buildId=1007325&view=logs&j=d1321064-f6c3-56b7-0172-6d994d01c836&t=39022612-2533-571e-7b41-94814e7d38bb&l=1339 ):

```python
command 'c++' failed: No such file or directory
```

On the one hand this is a true statement as conda-forge intentional uses [a triple naming scheme with compilers and associated tools]( https://github.com/conda-forge/ctng-compilers-feedstock/blob/8853ee7c0d15a9a73b35503c384f5894cf38c379/recipe/get_cpu_arch.sh#L18-L31 ) to include the architecture, OS type, and OS version. So technically `c++` does not exist. However what it points to is that a command in Distutils is not using the conda-forge compilers during the build, which is the real problem. Reading the CI log led to this code in CuPy as where the error arose

<hr>

# Aligning compilers with the Python interpreter build

Most of the time when an error like this shows up with `distutils` the answer is to ensure [`distutils.sysconfig`'s `customize_compiler`]( https://docs.python.org/3.11/distutils/apiref.html#distutils.sysconfig.customize_compiler ) is called on the new `CCompiler` object created by `distutils`. What this does (as the legacy `distutils` documentation in Python 3.11 linked above explains), is update the `distutils` `CCompiler` object based on settings used to build Python itself. IOW in conda-forge `python` is also built with conda-forge's compiler toolchain. So residual information from that build experience lives in the `python` package conda-forge produces. Thus `customize_compiler` then updates `CCompiler` to work with the conda-forge toolchain. Should add `customize_compiler` only does something in the UNIX case currently (it could potentially be updated to do something on Windows). This would be done by a change like that shown below. Normally this would be enough

```diff
     compiler = ccompiler.new_compiler()
+    distutils.sysconfig.customize_compiler(compiler)
```

<hr>

# Handling cross-compilers

However as the goal of the logic in this part of the CuPy code base is to build an artifact for the native platform and inspect it, that won't work with these compiler settings. Why is that? Well conda-forge uses a cross-compiling toolchain. Meaning the compilers are able to perform a build on a different platform than where the final package will be deployed. So building on Linux `x86_64` (plentiful in the world of CI currently) to create packages for Linux ARM (less available currently). IOW this is done to balance resource availability with user demand. So calling `customize_compiler` in conda-forge actually configures the compiler to target the platform where packages will be deployed (called `host_platform`). However for CuPy's use case here, we need to target `build_platform`, which may be different

As a result a manual patch is needed to configure the build tools for `build_platform`. The logic Leo added here did that based on [what `distutils` had in Python 3.11]( https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/795f2597a4be988e2bb19b69ff9958e981cb894e/Lib/distutils/unixccompiler.py#L49-L63 ). So what were we missing that resulted in new errors?

<hr>

# Setuptools, Distutils & the evolving Python packaging landscape

For a long time now, `setuptools` has monkeypatched `distutils`. In the past this meant there needed to be a specific `import` order for this to work. Namely `setuptools` first and then `distutils`. Though this caused some consternation for users and maintainers ( https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/issues/2259 ). So `setuptools` changed this to always prefer its own vendored `distutils` ( https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/pull/2305 ). Why does this matter? Well it means we never actually get `distutils` from the Python stdlib. We always get it from what `setuptools` vendored. This also explains why upstream removing its copy of `distutils` in Python 3.12 (please see [PEP 632]( https://peps.python.org/pep-0632 )) did not affect us (as we were not using it)

Though why do we care how `setuptools` supplies `distutils`. Well that [source code lives outside CPython]( https://github.com/pypa/distutils ) and we get updates whenever `setuptools` refreshes [its vendored `distutils`]( https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/tree/v72.0.0/setuptools/_distutils ). IOW just because the `distutils` source is updated doesn't mean it will be in `setuptools` yet. The vendored copy needs to be refreshed as well, which may happen out-of-sync relative to the `distutils` source. These pieces are important when trying to make sense of changes to `distutils` and how/when they will affect us

<hr>

# Distutils learns about C++ compilers

So what changed in `distutils`? Earlier this year `distutils` included updates for C++ compiler handling via PR ( https://github.com/pypa/distutils/pull/228 ). This expanded [the executables in `CCompiler`]( https://github.com/pypa/distutils/blob/fcef96367a0aef5bcf58027553ca36d50805210c/distutils/unixccompiler.py#L111-L129 ) by adding a C++ equivalent executable for every C associated one previously. This actually improves working with C++ compilers in `distutils`, which is pretty important for CUDA as well. So this is a helpful fix

<hr>

# Software supply chains and their logistics

Even though that `distutils` improvement went in at the beginning of August, we did not see it right away. This is because Setuptools refreshed `distutils` a week later with PR ( https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/pull/4539 ). The next Setuptools release, [72.2.0]( https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/releases/tag/v72.2.0 ), was a week after that (so 2 weeks after the `distutils` PR was merged). Then conda-forge waited a week to confirm there were not user errors showing up before shipping ( https://github.com/conda-forge/setuptools-feedstock/pull/355 ). So we only started seeing this error 3 weeks after the `distutils` change

<hr>

# Resolution

In any event, now that we have learned about these changes and understood the source, we can apply an appropriate patch. Namely...

* Configure the `distutils` `CCompiler` to match how Python is built
* Patch toolchain to target the build machine where it is run
* Make sure to cover the new Distutils C++ compilers

This PR includes these needed fixes for Setuptools https://github.com/cupy/cupy/issues/8541

# Background

Recently Leo added a patch to improve the Conda building experience in PR: https://github.com/cupy/cupy/pull/7603 (which is fantastic as this was [a large patch in the cupy-feedstock]( https://github.com/conda-forge/cupy-feedstock/blob/6db86965e825788262d22a5f3c8b9aa6b93f9d96/recipe/support_new_cuda_layout.diff ). Thanks Leo! 🙏)

This worked ok in our testing of CuPy 13.3.0 builds (not to mention previous CuPy builds where we applied this patch in conda-forge). However (as later identified after further investigation), this broke due to the recently released Setuptools [72.2.0]( https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/releases/tag/v72.2.0 ).

In particular the breakage resulted in the following error [on CI]( https://dev.azure.com/conda-forge/feedstock-builds/_build/results?buildId=1007325&view=logs&j=d1321064-f6c3-56b7-0172-6d994d01c836&t=39022612-2533-571e-7b41-94814e7d38bb&l=1339 ):

```python
command 'c++' failed: No such file or directory
```

On the one hand this is a true statement as conda-forge intentional uses [a triple naming scheme with compilers and associated tools]( https://github.com/conda-forge/ctng-compilers-feedstock/blob/8853ee7c0d15a9a73b35503c384f5894cf38c379/recipe/get_cpu_arch.sh#L18-L31 ) to include the architecture, OS type, and OS version. So technically `c++` does not exist. However what it points to is that a command in Distutils is not using the conda-forge compilers during the build, which is the real problem. Reading the CI log led to this code in CuPy as where the error arose

<hr>

# Aligning compilers with the Python interpreter build

Most of the time when an error like this shows up with `distutils` the answer is to ensure [`distutils.sysconfig`'s `customize_compiler`]( https://docs.python.org/3.11/distutils/apiref.html#distutils.sysconfig.customize_compiler ) is called on the new `CCompiler` object created by `distutils`. What this does (as the legacy `distutils` documentation in Python 3.11 linked above explains), is update the `distutils` `CCompiler` object based on settings used to build Python itself. IOW in conda-forge `python` is also built with conda-forge's compiler toolchain. So residual information from that build experience lives in the `python` package conda-forge produces. Thus `customize_compiler` then updates `CCompiler` to work with the conda-forge toolchain. Should add `customize_compiler` only does something in the UNIX case currently (it could potentially be updated to do something on Windows). This would be done by a change like that shown below. Normally this would be enough

```diff
     compiler = ccompiler.new_compiler()
+    distutils.sysconfig.customize_compiler(compiler)
```

<hr>

# Handling cross-compilers

However as the goal of the logic in this part of the CuPy code base is to build an artifact for the native platform and inspect it, that won't work with these compiler settings. Why is that? Well conda-forge uses a cross-compiling toolchain. Meaning the compilers are able to perform a build on a different platform than where the final package will be deployed. So building on Linux `x86_64` (plentiful in the world of CI currently) to create packages for Linux ARM (less available currently). IOW this is done to balance resource availability with user demand. So calling `customize_compiler` in conda-forge actually configures the compiler to target the platform where packages will be deployed (called `host_platform`). However for CuPy's use case here, we need to target `build_platform`, which may be different

As a result a manual patch is needed to configure the build tools for `build_platform`. The logic Leo added here did that based on [what `distutils` had in Python 3.11]( https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/795f2597a4be988e2bb19b69ff9958e981cb894e/Lib/distutils/unixccompiler.py#L49-L63 ). So what were we missing that resulted in new errors?

<hr>

# Setuptools, Distutils & the evolving Python packaging landscape

For a long time now, `setuptools` has monkeypatched `distutils`. In the past this meant there needed to be a specific `import` order for this to work. Namely `setuptools` first and then `distutils`. Though this caused some consternation for users and maintainers ( https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/issues/2259 ). So `setuptools` changed this to always prefer its own vendored `distutils` ( https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/pull/2305 ). Why does this matter? Well it means we never actually get `distutils` from the Python stdlib. We always get it from what `setuptools` vendored. This also explains why upstream removing its copy of `distutils` in Python 3.12 (please see [PEP 632]( https://peps.python.org/pep-0632 )) did not affect us (as we were not using it)

Though why do we care how `setuptools` supplies `distutils`. Well that [source code lives outside CPython]( https://github.com/pypa/distutils ) and we get updates whenever `setuptools` refreshes [its vendored `distutils`]( https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/tree/v72.0.0/setuptools/_distutils ). IOW just because the `distutils` source is updated doesn't mean it will be in `setuptools` yet. The vendored copy needs to be refreshed as well, which may happen out-of-sync relative to the `distutils` source. These pieces are important when trying to make sense of changes to `distutils` and how/when they will affect us

<hr>

# Distutils learns about C++ compilers

So what changed in `distutils`? Earlier this year `distutils` included updates for C++ compiler handling via PR ( https://github.com/pypa/distutils/pull/228 ). This expanded [the executables in `CCompiler`]( https://github.com/pypa/distutils/blob/fcef96367a0aef5bcf58027553ca36d50805210c/distutils/unixccompiler.py#L111-L129 ) by adding a C++ equivalent executable for every C associated one previously. This actually improves working with C++ compilers in `distutils`, which is pretty important for CUDA as well. So this is a helpful fix

<hr>

# Software supply chains and their logistics

Even though that `distutils` improvement went in at the beginning of August, we did not see it right away. This is because Setuptools refreshed `distutils` a week later with PR ( https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/pull/4539 ). The next Setuptools release, [72.2.0]( https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/releases/tag/v72.2.0 ), was a week after that (so 2 weeks after the `distutils` PR was merged). Then conda-forge waited a week to confirm there were not user errors showing up before shipping ( https://github.com/conda-forge/setuptools-feedstock/pull/355 ). So we only started seeing this error 3 weeks after the `distutils` change

<hr>

# Resolution

In any event, now that we have learned about these changes and understood the source, we can apply an appropriate patch. Namely...

* Configure the `distutils` `CCompiler` to match how Python is built
* Patch toolchain to target the build machine where it is run
* Make sure to cover the new Distutils C++ compilers

This PR includes these needed fixes for Setuptools 72.2.0","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/cupy/cupy/issues/8544/reactions","total_count":3,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":2,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/cupy/cupy/issues/8544/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/cupy/cupy/issues/comments/2401519679","html_url":"https://github.com/cupy/cupy/pull/8544#issuecomment-2401519679","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/cupy/cupy/issues/8544","id":2401519679,"node_id":"IC_kwDOBFKgkM6PJEg_","user":{"login":"kmaehashi","id":939877,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjkzOTg3Nw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/939877?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmaehashi","html_url":"https://github.com/kmaehashi","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmaehashi/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmaehashi/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmaehashi/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmaehashi/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmaehashi/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmaehashi/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmaehashi/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmaehashi/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmaehashi/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-09T07:13:33Z","updated_at":"2024-10-09T07:13:33Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"Thanks John, LGTM!","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/cupy/cupy/issues/comments/2401519679/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

What this does (as the legacy `distutils` documentation in Python 3.11 linked above explains), is update the `distutils` `CCompiler` object based on settings used to build Python itself. IOW in conda-forge `python` is also built with conda-forge's compiler toolchain. So residual information from that build experience lives in the `python` package conda-forge produces. Thus `customize_compiler` then updates `CCompiler` to work with the conda-forge toolchain. Should add `customize_compiler` only does something in the UNIX case currently (it could potentially be updated to do something on Windows). This would be done by a change like that shown below. Normally this would be enough

```diff
     compiler = ccompiler.new_compiler()
+    distutils.sysconfig.customize_compiler(compiler)
```

<hr>

# Handling cross-compilers

However as the goal of the logic in this part of the CuPy code base is to build an artifact for the native platform and inspect it, that won't work with these compiler settings. Why is that? Well conda-forge uses a cross-compiling toolchain. Meaning the compilers are able to perform a build on a different platform than where the final package will be deployed. So building on Linux `x86_64` (plentiful in the world of CI currently) to create packages for Linux ARM (less available currently). IOW this is done to balance resource availability with user demand. So calling `customize_compiler` in conda-forge actually configures the compiler to target the platform where packages will be deployed (called `host_platform`). However for CuPy's use case here, we need to target `build_platform`, which may be different

As a result a manual patch is needed to configure the build tools for `build_platform`. The logic Leo added here did that based on [what `distutils` had in Python 3.11]( https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/795f2597a4be988e2bb19b69ff9958e981cb894e/Lib/distutils/unixccompiler.py#L49-L63 ). So what were we missing that resulted in new errors?

<hr>

# Setuptools, Distutils & the evolving Python packaging landscape

For a long time now, `setuptools` has monkeypatched `distutils`. In the past this meant there needed to be a specific `import` order for this to work. Namely `setuptools` first and then `distutils`. Though this caused some consternation for users and maintainers ( https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/issues/2259 ). So `setuptools` changed this to always prefer its own vendored `distutils` ( https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/pull/2305 ). Why does this matter? Well it means we never actually get `distutils` from the Python stdlib. We always get it from what `setuptools` vendored. This also explains why upstream removing its copy of `distutils` in Python 3.12 (please see [PEP 632]( https://peps.python.org/pep-0632 )) did not affect us (as we were not using it)

Though why do we care how `setuptools` supplies `distutils`. Well that [source code lives outside CPython]( https://github.com/pypa/distutils ) and we get updates whenever `setuptools` refreshes [its vendored `distutils`]( https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/tree/v72.0.0/setuptools/_distutils ). IOW just because the `distutils` source is updated doesn't mean it will be in `setuptools` yet. The vendored copy needs to be refreshed as well, which may happen out-of-sync relative to the `distutils` source. These pieces are important when trying to make sense of changes to `distutils` and how/when they will affect us

<hr>

# Distutils learns about C++ compilers

So what changed in `distutils`? Earlier this year `distutils` included updates for C++ compiler handling via PR ( https://github.com/pypa/distutils/pull/228 ). This expanded [the executables in `CCompiler`]( https://github.com/pypa/distutils/blob/fcef96367a0aef5bcf58027553ca36d50805210c/distutils/unixccompiler.py#L111-L129 ) by adding a C++ equivalent executable for every C associated one previously. This actually improves working with C++ compilers in `distutils`, which is pretty important for CUDA as well. So this is a helpful fix

<hr>

# Software supply chains and their logistics

Even though that `distutils` improvement went in at the beginning of August, we did not see it right away. This is because Setuptools refreshed `distutils` a week later with PR ( https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/pull/4539 ). The next Setuptools release, [72.2.0]( https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/releases/tag/v72.2.0 ), was a week after that (so 2 weeks after the `distutils` PR was merged). Then conda-forge waited a week to confirm there were not user errors showing up before shipping ( https://github.com/conda-forge/setuptools-feedstock/pull/355 ). So we only started seeing this error 3 weeks after the `distutils` change

<hr>

# Resolution

In any event, now that we have learned about these changes and understood the source, we can apply an appropriate patch. Namely...

* Configure the `distutils` `CCompiler` to match how Python is built
* Patch toolchain to target the build machine where it is run
* Make sure to cover the new Distutils C++ compilers

This PR includes these needed fixes for Setuptools drop it. Distutils sets `preprocessor` to `None` anyways. Meaning

believe the issue is that `platform.processor()` returns an empty string on some systems (https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/platform.html#platform.processor), and as such on my machine in a Conda env the `cupy._environment._get_include_dir_from_conda_or_wheel` method returns an invalid path:
```
>>> from cupy import _environment
>>> _environment._get_include_dir_from_conda_or_wheel(12, 5)
['/home/dagardner/work/conda/envs/morpheus/targets/-linux/include', '/home/dagardner/work/conda/envs/morpheus/include']
```

One possible work-around would be to call `platform.machine()` on systems that return an empty string:
```
>>> import platform
>>> platform.processor()
''
>>> platform.machine()
'x86_64'
```

Repro:
```
import cupy as cp
cp.array(cp.zeros((1,)))
```

Description

Currently, `NpzFile` returned from `cupy.load` does not implement `Mapping` protocol (e.g., `get()`, `items()`, etc. are missing). It would be better to support these methods.

CuPy: https://github.com/cupy/cupy/blob/v13.3.0/cupy/_io/npz.py#L11-L29
NumPy: https://numpy.org/devdocs/reference/generated/numpy.lib.npyio.NpzFile.html

Also it looks NumPy 2.0 made the class documented. We should follow that as well.

### Additional Information

I noticed this when porting [llama3.np](https://github.com/likejazz/llama3.np) for CuPy. If this is implemented, `llama3.np` will run on CuPy just by rewriting `import numpy as np` to `import cupy as {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/cupy/cupy/issues/4775","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/cupy/cupy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/cupy/cupy/issues/4775/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/cupy/cupy/issues/4775/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/cupy/cupy/issues/4775/events","html_url":"https://github.com/cupy/cupy/issues/4775","id":818848041,"node_id":"MDU6SXNzdWU4MTg4NDgwNDE=","number":4775,"title":"Pinned memory leak","user":{"login":"nizamov","id":12709452,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyNzA5NDUy","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/12709452?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/nizamov","html_url":"https://github.com/nizamov","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nizamov/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nizamov/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nizamov/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nizamov/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nizamov/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nizamov/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nizamov/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nizamov/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nizamov/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":1360743887,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxMzYwNzQzODg3","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/cupy/cupy/labels/issue-checked","name":"issue-checked","color":"ffffff","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2021-03-01T13:05:43Z","updated_at":"2024-10-10T09:38:31Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Hi, there

seems that I have found some kind of a strange memory allocation bug. I am running Debian testing, which hast cuda 11.2 and cython 29.21. So I had to build cupy ("pip3 install cupy" - no errors reported except missing cudnn/cublas etc).

Now, the problem is that I wanted to benchmark memory transfer between host memory and device memory. In general, this is nothing more than measuring the timing of many transfers of the same array. So memory allocation had to be really negligible, but with cupy it is not. It is growing until it gets killed by OOM-killer. I had the similar test in Numba without any problems. 

Here is a simplified code that triggers such behavior on my PC (quite an old Xeon E3-1220 with 16Gb DDR3 memory, python 3.9.1).  If there is no explicit release of memory buffers, the memory occupied by python grows steadily. Actually it should not. I have added a small time delay to the cycle to be able to observe this behavior and found another strange thing. If time delay is longer than 0.2s (I suspect that the value depends on the hardware), then the memory occupation gets capped at 3.4 Gb and test passes. For comparison, with pinned_mempool.free_all_blocks() memory occupation remains at the level around 2.4Gb. 

It seems to me, that there is some automatic garbage collection which can not cope with a rate that this test allocates/deallocates pinned memory. But works to at least some extent, if the rate is decreased by addition of sufficient sleeping time. Since I do not observe such problem with Numba on the same configuration, I guess that this bug is not in linux/python/cuda stack but rather in cupy itself.  



```
import numpy as np
import cupy as cp
import time

N = 10*1024*1024

cpu_data = np.zeros(N, dtype=np.float32)

mempool = cp.get_default_memory_pool()
pinned_mempool = cp.get_default_pinned_memory_pool()

for i in range(5000):
    gpu_data = cp.asarray(cpu_data)

    if i % 100 == 0:
        print(i, mempool.used_bytes(), mempool.total_bytes(), pinned_mempool.n_free_blocks())    

        time.sleep(0.1)
        #pinned_mempool.free_all_blocks()

print("Ok")

```

Message in dmesg when test gets killed:

```
Out of memory: Killed process 7203 (python3) total-vm:17809796kB, anon-rss:115924kB, file-rss:75444kB, shmem-rss:11151360kB, pgtables:22756kB oom_score_adj:0
oom_reaper: reaped process 7203 (python3), now anon-rss:0kB, file-rss:74732kB, shmem-rss:11151360kB

```


Output of `python -c 'import cupy; cupy.show_config()'`

+    HMM stands for heterogeneous memory management. It is a kernel-level

system allocated memory (SAM). This is a new way to allocate device-accessible memory in two environments:
* HMM: Heterogeneous Memory Management, a software solution to support SAM in x86 PCIe based systems.
* ATS: Address Translation Service, a hardware solution on the Grace Hopper superchip.

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/cupy/cupy/pulls/comments/1795648049","pull_request_review_id":2360319614,"id":1795648049,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOBFKgkM5rB2ox","diff_hunk":"@@ -243,6 +263,89 @@ cdef class ManagedMemory(BaseMemory):
             runtime.free(self.ptr)
 
 
+@cython.no_gc
+cdef class SystemMemory(BaseMemory):
+    """Memory allocation on an HMM/ATS enabled system.
+
+    HMM stands for heterogeneous memory management. It is a kernel-level
+    feature allowing memory allocated via the system ``malloc`` to be
+    accessible by both CPU and GPU.
+
+    ATS stands for Address Translation Services. It is a hardware/software
+    feature on Grace Hopper that enables the CPU and GPU to share a single
+    per-process page table, allowing memory allocated by the system to be
+    accessible by both CPU and GPU.
+
+    This class provides an RAII interface of the memory allocation.
+
+    Args:
+        size (int): Size of the memory allocation in bytes.
+    """
+
+    def __init__(self, size_t size):
+        self.size = size
+        # TODO(leofang): using the GPU id may not be ideal, but setting it
+        # to cudaCpuDeviceId (-1) would require a lot of changes
+        self.device_id = device.get_device_id()
+        self.ptr = 0
+        if size > 0:
+            self.ptr = <intptr_t>c_malloc(size)
+        self._owner = None","path":"cupy/cuda/memory.pyx","commit_id":"7bcf7b163962921af35c5c9e26aa132c60628ffa","original_commit_id":"7bcf7b163962921af35c5c9e26aa132c60628ffa","user":{"login":"kmaehashi","id":939877,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjkzOTg3Nw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/939877?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmaehashi","html_url":"https://github.com/kmaehashi","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmaehashi/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmaehashi/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmaehashi/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmaehashi/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmaehashi/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmaehashi/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmaehashi/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmaehashi/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmaehashi/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"I think it's better to have another flag to indicate the ownership of the pointer.
